title: CPVO BoA A005/2002 - Santis 99
text:
European Union
Community Plant Variety Office
Case A 005/2002
DECISION
Concerning the Appeal lodged by the Applicant
Novartis Seeds B.V., succeeded in title by
Syngenta Seeds B.V.
Westeinde 62, P.O. Box 2, 1600 AA Enkhuizen, The Netherlands
Representative: Jolanda Dekker, c/o Syngenta
further party of the proceedings
Community Plant Variety Office
Representative: President B. Kiewit
Relating to Community plant variety right application No 1999/1569
Variety denomination:
SANTIS 99
Species: Sanvitalia Lam.
On 2 April 2003, the Board of Appeal of the Community Plant Variety Office, composed
of G. Winkler (Chairman), C.J. Barendrecht and A. Bould (Members),
gave the following decision:

- 2 -
The appeal is refused
Facts
On 5 November 1999 Novartis Seed B.V., predecessor in title of the appellant, filed an
application for a Community plant variety right for the denomination SANTIS 99, which
belongs to the species Sanvitalia Lam.
The variety has been marketed since 30 November 1998 under the denomination
CUZCO in both The Netherlands and outside the territory of the European Union in the
USA.
The technical examination was carried out by the Bundessortenamt in
Hanover, Germany. The report of the technical examination, dated 15 August 2001,
concluded that the candidate variety was not distinct from the reference variety
"Aztekengold". According to an annex to the report the company Grünewald has
marketed the variety "Aztekengold" since 1997. This variety was first mentioned in
Grünewald's 1996/1997 catalogue, which the Bundessortenamt received on
2 June 1997.
Originally the variety Aztekengold was propagated by seed but Grünewald produced
clones by vegetative propagation and sold the material as rooted and unrooted cuttings.
During her visit on 16 July 2001 to the premises of the Bundessortenamt,
Mrs. Groneman of Novartis Seeds B.V. stated, that according to information available to
her on the date of the application of SANTIS 99, there was no Sanvitalia variety on the
market with dark green foliage and compact growth habit, which are the particular
characteristics of SANTIS 99.
According to a statement in the annex referred to above the Bundessortenamt was not
able to prove, that the material supplied by Grünewald in 2001 for the purpose of testing
was identical to the material, which had been sold in 1997, because this material had
not been included in the Sanvitalia trial.
During his visit to the premises of the Bundessortenamt on 10 August 2001,
Mr. Schraeder, manager of Grünewald, declared, that the material supplied to the
Bundessortenamt was identical to the material offered for sale since 1997.

- 3 -
In its decision No R 220 taken on 21 January 2002 the competent Committee of the
Community Plant Variety Office (hereinafter: the Office) refuses the plant variety
application based on Art. 61 (2) lit. b), Art. 57 (1), Art. 7 (1) of Council Regulation (EC)
No 2100/94 of 27 July 1994 on Community plant variety rights (hereinafter: CR). It was
held that the candidate variety was not sufficiently distinct from the reference variety
"Aztekengold".
On 20 March 2002, the successor in title of the applicant lodged an appeal against the
decision and duly paid the appeal fees.
As the Office did not grant interlocutory revision it remitted the case to the Board of
Appeal in accordance with Art. 70 (2) CR.
The appellant alleged, that the variety taken as reference by the testing authority was not
the same as the one mentioned in Grünewald's 1996/1997 catalogue and sold by
Grünewald before the date of its application for SANTIS 99. The earlier existence of
Aztekengold propagated by seed is not challenged, as the candidate variety originates
from a single plant that was selected from Aztekengold propagated by seed. This
selection took place under special conditions and the material was chosen for its
particular dark and compact appearance. On the date of the application for SANTIS 99
no such dark and compact variety was on the market.
As the technical examination did not start until 2001 there was ample time to copy the
candidate. As it is reproduced by cuttings, copying is not too difficult or time consuming.
The appellant believes the version of Grünewald's "Aztekengold" to be an illegal copy of
SANTIS 99.
The appellant requested its arguments be taken into consideration for a final decision.
The appellant was duly summoned to the oral hearing on 2 April 2003 before the Board
of Appeal but did not appear.
The President of the Community Plant Variety Office applied

- 4 -
for the dismissal of the appeal.
He argued that the appellant's allegation that Grünewald copied the candidate variety is
not substantiated. The fact of the disposal of the reference variety in
Grünewald's 1996/1997 catalogue coupled with the statement by Mr. Schraeder was
sufficient evidence that Grünewald's "Aztekengold" was a matter of common knowledge
on the date of the application of the candidate. In this connection it is not relevant
whether the reference variety, named "Aztekengold" by Grünewald, is true to type for the
seed-propagated form or a variant clone arising from propagation by cuttings. However,
if the latter, it should have had a different denomination to avoid confusion.
Grounds
The appeal is admissible but not well founded.
The disputed decision stands up to legal examination since the variety SANTIS 99 has
not been shown to be distinct from the reference variety "Aztekengold" by Grünewald
(Art. 61 (2) lit. b), Art. 7 (1) CR).
In accordance with Art. 7 (1) CR a variety shall be deemed to be distinct, if it is clearly
distinguishable by reference to the expression of the characteristics that results from a
particular genotype or combination of genotypes from any other variety whose existence
is a matter of common knowledge on the date of application determined pursuant to
Art. 51 CR.
The reference variety, of which Grünewald supplied testing material, was named
"Aztekengold" by Grünewald.
The candidate was not compared with the original seed-propagated variety
Aztekengold, which is indisputably a matter of common knowledge, but with Grünewald's
so-called "Aztekengold" obtained by vegetative propagation from Aztekengold.

- 5 -
It can be left undecided whether the reference variety is rightly named Aztekengold or
not. The relevant point is whether (Grünewald's) "Aztekengold" was a variety in common
knowledge on the date of the application of SANTIS 99.
Grünewald's "Aztekengold" was already disposed of in its 1996/1997 catalogue.
There is no reasonable doubt about the identity of the material offered in the
1996/1997 catalogue and the material supplied in 2001 by Grünewald.
The appellant contested this fact only by believing it to be an illegal copy of SANTIS 99.
This argument is nothing but an expression of an insufficiently founded suspicion. Only
the vague opportunity of illegal reproduction of SANTIS 99 between 1996/1997 and
2001 is not sufficient to raise relevant doubts about the identity of the materials. The
serious suspicion uttered by the appellant against Grünewald does not corroborate this
grave accusation, even if it is taken in consideration that Mr. Schraeder was interested
in the rejection of the application of SANTIS 99, as the appellant did not produce any
further facts that could support his point of view.
Also the statement of Mrs. Gronemann, that according to her knowledge the candidate
variety was not on the market on the date of the application, is not suffiently
substantiated. Certainly it is to be assumed that competitors, including the appellant,
observe the market very attentively, but Mrs. Gronemann did not mention in detail what
kind of information was available to her, particularly whether she knew about the
1996/1997 catalogue and the characteristics of the "Aztekengold" sold by Grünewald.
Therefore the defence arguments presented by the appellant are not sufficient to
question the contested decision.
The result of the report on technical examination with reference to the lack of
distinctness is undisputed.
Winkler Barendrecht
